summary:
The Harifian culture was a late Epipaleolithic hunter-gatherer tradition of the Negev Desert, Sinai, and adjacent arid regions of the southern Levant, dated to approximately 9600–8500 BCE. Named after the site of Abu Salem (Har Harif) in the central Negev, the Harifian is characterized by a distinctive microlithic tool industry including the eponymous Harifian point — a small, finely worked arrowhead with a distinctive burin facet — alongside bladelets and backed tools.

Harifian communities were mobile hunter-gatherers adapted to the arid and semi-arid environments of the southern Levant, representing a cultural tradition distinct from but broadly contemporary with the early farming communities developing in the more productive Jordan Valley and coastal Levant to the north. The Harifian is associated with the semi-desert periphery of the early Neolithic zone, where the agricultural transition was slower to take hold due to limited rainfall and resources. Harifian communities maintained contacts with their agricultural and pastoral neighbors, as evidenced by some shared artifact types, but preserved a mobile foraging lifestyle well into the period when farming villages were well established elsewhere in the Levant.
